emergent_themes.description: PhD students experience unique tension where food needs interrupt academic productivity, leading to frustration with hunger as a distraction from work
Despite price sensitivity, decision fatigue and hunger urgency override rational price comparison, leading to ordering even when costs feel too high
Subscriptions create psychological permission to order more frequently, ultimately increasing total spending despite perceived savings
Negative experiences with one platform create lasting preference for competitors, with trust becoming a key differentiator

coding_summary: P034 reveals the complex psychology of a PhD student balancing academic pressure, financial constraints, and food convenience. Key insights include: (1) Academic productivity creates unique food stress where hunger is viewed as interruption rather than natural need, (2) Decision fatigue consistently overrides stated price sensitivity, leading to ordering despite high costs, (3) Strong preference for Deliveroo over Uber Eats due to trust and reliability differences, (4) Clear desire for better grocery delivery options that compete with Ocado, (5) Post-graduation expectations of more purposeful, less frequent ordering focused on social occasions.

coverage_gaps.reason: Participant showed no evidence of stigma around solo ordering - appears comfortable with individual food decisions and doesn't express shame about ordering alone
